EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow corridor of elevated instability around 1000 J/kg combined with effective shear of 30-35 knots in the exit region of the low-level jet to produce scattered thunderstorms overnight.  A few briefly stronger updrafts produced hail, with isolated reports up to quarter size.

Scattered thunderstorms developed near a cold front across southeast South Dakota during the evening. With MUCAPE around 1000 J/kg and decent deep-layer wind shear focused near the boundary, a few storms organized into loose clusters with bowing structures.
A short-duration bow echo evolved from thunderstorms which developed on the warm side of a stationary frontal boundary in the presence of weak shear and strong DCAPE.

A narrow zone of enhanced instability developed ahead of a cold front with up to 1500-2000 J/kg MLCAPE.  As a mid-level jet streak crossed the boundary in the late afternoon and evening, deep layer shear increased to around 50 knots. Isolated thunderstorms developed supercell characteristics, resulting in large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Emergency Manager estimated wind gusts as high as 80 to 90 mph.

An isolated thunderstorm within an environment with a well-defined inverted-V sounding produced a downburst wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust was measured by South Dakota RWIS near Cavour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed along a frontal boundary in the presence of 40 knots of deep-layer shear, which resulted in a briefly rotating storm producing marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly pea size, but there were a few stones up to ping-pong ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed along a frontal boundary in the presence of 40 knots of deep-layer shear, which resulted in a briefly rotating storm producing marginally large hail.
A strong thunderstorm developed along a frontal boundary in the presence of 40 knots of deep-layer shear, which resulted in a briefly rotating storm producing marginally large hail.
Clusters of thunderstorms developed along an advancing cold front, some of which produced small hail and sub-severe wind gusts.

A line of thunderstorms developed along a cold front as it moved out of central South Dakota.  With 1000-1500 J/kg MLCAPE and 40 to 45 knots of deep-layer shear, an isolated supercell developed on the south end of the line and resulted in spotty large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated from damage to small tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in warm advection ahead of a strong upper-level wave.  Isolated stronger cells produced hail up to penny size with fairly limited instability.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few tree branches up to 2 inches in diameter were also downed by winds estimated at less than 50 mph.

An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota.  An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on Sentinel satellite imagery, the tornado spun up in Turner County around 2 miles west southwest of Lennox in a field southwest of the intersection of 464th Avenue and 279th Street. The tornado tracked east-northeast for around one-half mile and crossed into Lincoln County just south of the intersection of 279th Street and 464th Avenue. After another half mile, the tornado uprooted seven large cottonwood trees and damaged numerous others at the Lenkota Country Club.  The tornado continued into the western portions of Lennox north of the city park, with a majority of damage to large trees between 4th and 5th Avenue as the path turned more eastward and eventually a bit southeastward.  The most significant damage occurred as the tornado approached State Highway 17, with a trampoline deposited high in a gnarled tree along South Cherry Street, and the complete destruction of a shed just east of Highway 17.  The tornado lifted about a quarter mile east of Highway 17, just east of Lennox.  Areas south of the path experienced strong rear-flank winds. While in Turner County, the tornado was rated EFU, while in Lincoln County it was rated EF1. The average path width for the tornado was 100 yards. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions continued throughout April, especially over north-central South Dakota. Temperatures started off April extraordinarily warm, with highs in the 70s and 80s. Temperatures cooled for the rest of the month, with averages around or just below normal. The cooler temperatures, combined with periodic precipitation events, prevented any large scale worsening or expansion of drought conditions. Precipitation throughout the month averaged one to one and a half inches below normal. 90-day deficits were as much as 2 to 4 inches, with 180-day deficits ranging from 2 to 4 inches as well. Soil moisture and stock ponds continued to be depleted. The Grassland Fire Danger reached high to very high several times throughout the month, with a handful of grass fires occurring. Several counties enacted burn bans.

A strong cold front brought gusty winds during the late evening into the overnight hours across portions of western South Dakota. Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were recorded north and east of Rapid City.

A strong cold front brought gusty winds during the late evening into the overnight hours across portions of western South Dakota. Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were recorded north and east of Rapid City.

A strong upper level disturbance brought rain and snow to much of western South Dakota. Temperatures were cold enough for heavy, wet snow over the Black Hills and parts of the foothills and adjacent plains. Snowfall was heaviest over parts of the central and southern Black Hills, where a band of heavy snow lingered for several hours. Ten to 18 inches of snow were reported from Nemo to Hill City to south of Custer. Elsewhere across the Black Hills into the foothills and parts of far southern South Dakota, five to ten inches of snow fell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts at around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent and persistent outbreak of Arctic air affected the entire region from February 6th through the 17th. The coldest days of the outbreak for many occurred Valentine's Day weekend, when high temperatures averaged around ten below zero, in northeastern South Dakota, to the single digits above zero, in central South Dakota. On February 14th, low temperatures dropped into the 20s to the 30s degrees below zero range. Extreme wind chills of 35 degrees to 55 degrees below zero also occurred on several days during the outbreak.|  |The magnitude of the cold during this outbreak was fairly rare compared to the past 50 years, at least in terms of the persistence of the Arctic air. This was especially impressive considering the lack of deep, fresh snow cover across most of the area. If there had been widespread deep, fresh snowpack ahead of this Arctic outbreak, low temperatures would have been more severe and more often approaching record territory. Even with the lack of snow cover, Pierre dropped to 20 degrees below zero or colder on the 14th and 15th. Since 1934, Pierre has had two other occurrences when the low temperature fell to 20 degrees below zero with no snow cover. The previous days were January 24th, 1961, and January 5th, 1979.||Impacts from this extreme and persistent cold include many frozen and/or broken water pipes (the limited snow depth did not help in this regard) and froze-over home sewer vents, dead vehicle batteries, school delays, and church cancellations. The prolonged cold caused significant strains to the power grid as demand spiked both locally and across several other states. Thousands of customers were at least briefly without power locally, particularly during the morning of Tue, Feb 16th. Concerns for rolling blackouts lingered for several days in this regard due to the continued extreme demand/strain, and people were repeatedly asked to conserve energy however possible.

Severe drought conditions developed in late February across much of north central and parts of central South Dakota.  Precipitation departures since October 1st, 2020 had been from three-quarters of an inch to two and a quarter inches below normal or only 25 to 50 percent of normal.  Topsoil and subsoil moistures were anywhere 17 percent to 45 percent short or very short. The winter wheat was showing the affects from the dry conditions with 1 percent to 43 percent very poor to fair. Some winter pastures were looking overgrazed with some ranchers bringing in supplemental feed. Some dugouts and stock ponds were dry or contained poor water quality.  Finally, cured fuels and lack of snow cover led to increased fire danger which was unusual for this time of year. The grassland fire danger had reached high to very high several times during the latter part of February, with a few grass fires reported.

An Arctic air mass settled over the Northern Plains for a prolonged period, producing bitterly cold temperatures and extremely low wind chills. Low temperatures were well below zero across the area, with wind chills as low as 35 below to 50 below zero.

A potent and persistent outbreak of Arctic air affected the entire region from February 6th through the 17th. The coldest days of the outbreak for many occurred Valentine's Day weekend, when high temperatures averaged around ten below zero, in northeastern South Dakota, to the single digits above zero, in central South Dakota. On February 14th, low temperatures dropped into the 20s to the 30s degrees below zero range. Extreme wind chills of 35 degrees to 55 degrees below zero also occurred on several days during the outbreak.|  |The magnitude of the cold during this outbreak was fairly rare compared to the past 50 years, at least in terms of the persistence of the Arctic air. This was especially impressive considering the lack of deep, fresh snow cover across most of the area. If there had been widespread deep, fresh snowpack ahead of this Arctic outbreak, low temperatures would have been more severe and more often approaching record territory. Even with the lack of snow cover, Pierre dropped to 20 degrees below zero or colder on the 14th and 15th. Since 1934, Pierre has had two other occurrences when the low temperature fell to 20 degrees below zero with no snow cover. The previous days were January 24th, 1961, and January 5th, 1979.||Impacts from this extreme and persistent cold include many frozen and/or broken water pipes (the limited snow depth did not help in this regard) and froze-over home sewer vents, dead vehicle batteries, school delays, and church cancellations. The prolonged cold caused significant strains to the power grid as demand spiked both locally and across several other states. Thousands of customers were at least briefly without power locally, particularly during the morning of Tue, Feb 16th. Concerns for rolling blackouts lingered for several days in this regard due to the continued extreme demand/strain, and people were repeatedly asked to conserve energy however possible.

Severe drought conditions developed in late February across much of north central and parts of central South Dakota.  Precipitation departures since October 1st, 2020 had been from three-quarters of an inch to two and a quarter inches below normal or only 25 to 50 percent of normal.  Topsoil and subsoil moistures were anywhere 17 percent to 45 percent short or very short. The winter wheat was showing the affects from the dry conditions with 1 percent to 43 percent very poor to fair. Some winter pastures were looking overgrazed with some ranchers bringing in supplemental feed. Some dugouts and stock ponds were dry or contained poor water quality.  Finally, cured fuels and lack of snow cover led to increased fire danger which was unusual for this time of year. The grassland fire danger had reached high to very high several times during the latter part of February, with a few grass fires reported.

Elsewhere, a few inches of snow were reported.

A massive arctic plunge into the Northern Plains brought almost a week of dangerous and life threatening wind chills to the region. The worst conditions were recorded between February 13 and February 16 when wind chills occasionally dropped as low as 35 below to 55 below zero.  Numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures were also established.  The areal extent of the extreme cold in the central portions of the U.S. led to stress on the power grid, which necessitated several short-term rolling power outages impacting more than 10,000 customers during the peak of the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During several periods over three days with wind chill below -35, values reached a minimum of -42 5 miles west of Geddes, -39 10 miles west of Platte, and -37 at Platte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent and persistent outbreak of Arctic air affected the entire region from February 6th through the 17th. The coldest days of the outbreak for many occurred Valentine's Day weekend, when high temperatures averaged around ten below zero, in northeastern South Dakota, to the single digits above zero, in central South Dakota. On February 14th, low temperatures dropped into the 20s to the 30s degrees below zero range. Extreme wind chills of 35 degrees to 55 degrees below zero also occurred on several days during the outbreak.|  |The magnitude of the cold during this outbreak was fairly rare compared to the past 50 years, at least in terms of the persistence of the Arctic air. This was especially impressive considering the lack of deep, fresh snow cover across most of the area. If there had been widespread deep, fresh snowpack ahead of this Arctic outbreak, low temperatures would have been more severe and more often approaching record territory. Even with the lack of snow cover, Pierre dropped to 20 degrees below zero or colder on the 14th and 15th. Since 1934, Pierre has had two other occurrences when the low temperature fell to 20 degrees below zero with no snow cover. The previous days were January 24th, 1961, and January 5th, 1979.||Impacts from this extreme and persistent cold include many frozen and/or broken water pipes (the limited snow depth did not help in this regard) and froze-over home sewer vents, dead vehicle batteries, school delays, and church cancellations. The prolonged cold caused significant strains to the power grid as demand spiked both locally and across several other states. Thousands of customers were at least briefly without power locally, particularly during the morning of Tue, Feb 16th. Concerns for rolling blackouts lingered for several days in this regard due to the continued extreme demand/strain, and people were repeatedly asked to conserve energy however possible.

Severe drought conditions developed in late February across much of north central and parts of central South Dakota.  Precipitation departures since October 1st, 2020 had been from three-quarters of an inch to two and a quarter inches below normal or only 25 to 50 percent of normal.  Topsoil and subsoil moistures were anywhere 17 percent to 45 percent short or very short. The winter wheat was showing the affects from the dry conditions with 1 percent to 43 percent very poor to fair. Some winter pastures were looking overgrazed with some ranchers bringing in supplemental feed. Some dugouts and stock ponds were dry or contained poor water quality.  Finally, cured fuels and lack of snow cover led to increased fire danger which was unusual for this time of year. The grassland fire danger had reached high to very high several times during the latter part of February, with a few grass fires reported.

A band of snowfall developed within a confluent trough across the region. Strong mid-level frontogenesis and slight instability above the frontal zone produced banding, which was transient and limited snowfall amounts to generally 2 to 6 inches. Outside the heavier snowfall, some light mixed-type precipitation occurred, but had limited impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was heavy at times between 0100-0600CST, and produced treacherous travel conditions.  Snowfall totaled 6.0 inches 8 miles south of Brookings and 4.7 inches at Flandreau.

A passing cold front brought wind gusts over 50 mph to much of northwestern to west central South Dakota. The strongest winds were recorded over Harding County and especially in the Buffalo area, where gusts of 60 to 70 mph occurred during the overnight hours.

A very strong cold front was accompanied by a narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall, as well as wind gusting to 30 to 45 mph.  Most areas saw a brief period of rain, freezing rain or sleet before changing to snow, which was accompanied by a temperature fall of 10 to 20 degrees over a six hour period.  Contraction of the band occurred due to strong elevated instability, with isolated thunder occurring in southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.  While most areas received around an inch or two of snowfall, the band peaked in intensity around the Sioux Falls area with up to three to five inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was briefly heavy and totaled 2.1 inches 4 miles south southwest of Pickstown.  Roads became hazardous, and frequent wind gusts as high as 37 mph at Fairfax produced several hours of blowing snow with visibility as low as one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several periods of dense freezing fog from late January into early February brought heavy riming/ice buildup on power lines across much of north central South Dakota.  The heavy riming/ice buildup caused utility poles to lean, lines to sag, and wires to come into contact and fail.  Most of the outages were west of Highway 281 to the Missouri River.  The rime/ice buildup in some places was the circumference of a quart jar weighting down the lines close enough to the ground to touch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved out of central South Dakota into the James River Valley late in the evening.  While winds were generally from 40 to 50 mph, one stronger cell produced a severe wind gust near Huron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was measured by automated station KHON.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnal heating contributed to strong 0-3km CAPE, as moisture quickly pooled near a slowly moving frontal zone across southeast South Dakota.  A brief landspout developed during the updraft phase of stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout spun up just south of the intersection of 305th Street and 454th Avenue south southwest of Wakonda.  The tornado was witnessed intermittently touching down over the next 5 minutes, which kicked up dust while it moved across open fields. The average path width was 25 yards. Tornado timing, path, and width based on eyewitness accounts and video and photographic evidence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed near a cold front across southeast South Dakota during the evening. With MUCAPE around 1000 J/kg and decent deep-layer wind shear focused near the boundary, a few storms organized into loose clusters with bowing structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout was witnessed by a Lake County deputy for around 5 minutes. The weak tornado touched down around a mile south of Winfred in a lowland area, and tracked east northeast through open wetlands and fields, and produced no damage while only dirt was lofted.  The tornado lifted south of State Highway 34 southwest of the intersection with 446th Avenue.  The maximum path width during the track was 25 yards. Tornado timing, path, and width based on eyewitness accounts and video evidence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive arctic plunge into the Northern Plains brought almost a week of dangerous and life threatening wind chills to the region. The worst conditions were recorded between February 13 and February 16 when wind chills occasionally dropped as low as 35 below to 55 below zero.  Numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures were also established.  The areal extent of the extreme cold in the central portions of the U.S. led to stress on the power grid, which necessitated several short-term rolling power outages impacting more than 10,000 customers during the peak of the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During several periods over three days with wind chill below -35, values reached a minimum of -39 at Mitchell and -38 at Ethan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snowfall developed within a confluent trough across the region. Strong mid-level frontogenesis and slight instability above the frontal zone produced banding, which was transient and limited snowfall amounts to generally 2 to 6 inches. Outside the heavier snowfall, some light mixed-type precipitation occurred, but had limited impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches 5 miles northeast of Salem and 6 miles northwest of Montrose,  and resulted in dangerous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several periods of dense freezing fog from late January into early February brought heavy riming/ice buildup on power lines across much of north central South Dakota.  The heavy riming/ice buildup caused utility poles to lean, lines to sag, and wires to come into contact and fail.  Most of the outages were west of Highway 281 to the Missouri River.  The rime/ice buildup in some places was the circumference of a quart jar weighting down the lines close enough to the ground to touch.

A Hitchcock South Dakota man became disoriented in dense fog Tuesday night, February 2nd while driving a snowmobile four miles north of Willow Lake in Clark county. The man had gone ice fishing and was reported missing at 6 am on Wednesday, February 3rd. The man drove his snowmobile into open water on the north side of Dry Lake and drowned.  The Codington County Search and Rescue dive team recovered the man's body from the open water at around 6 pm Wednesday, February 3rd.

A very strong cold front was accompanied by a narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall, as well as wind gusting to 30 to 45 mph.  Most areas saw a brief period of rain, freezing rain or sleet before changing to snow, which was accompanied by a temperature fall of 10 to 20 degrees over a six hour period.  Contraction of the band occurred due to strong elevated instability, with isolated thunder occurring in southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.  While most areas received around an inch or two of snowfall, the band peaked in intensity around the Sioux Falls area with up to three to five inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was briefly heavy and totaled 2.0 inches 6 miles north northeast of Elk Point.  Roads became hazardous, and frequent wind gusts as high as 43 mph 3 miles south of Beresford produced several hours of blowing snow with visibility as low as one-half mile.

Thunderstorms produced heavy rains of 2 to 6 inches across a large portion of central and western Corson County (isolated 6 to 10 inches) during severe to extreme drought on the evening of June 8th. This caused the Grand River at Little Eagle to rise above flood stage, 15 feet, from the early morning of the 10th to the early morning of the 11th. Starting at about 5 feet, the river crested in moderate flood state at 17.89 feet by the early afternoon on the 10th.

A large slow-moving area of low pressure wandered from Colorado to eastern South Dakota over a two-day period. Precipitation circulated back into southeast South Dakota, which started as a period of rainfall during the morning and early afternoon on March 14.  The rain changed to snow most quickly in south central SD by midday, and slowest toward Interstate 29 which occurred during the evening. Snow became heavy at times during the later evening and overnight, with snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Tyndall and 8 miles west northwest of Springfield. Travel become hazardous as snow rates increased at night.

A low pressure system moved across the Central Plains, producing areas of heavy snow across southwestern South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell from far southwestern into west central South Dakota, where snowfall was generally four to eight inches. Local amounts around a foot fell in the heaviest band across eastern Oglala Lakota and western Jackson Counties. Lesser amounts fell over the rest of southwestern South Dakota.

A significant wind event occurred from the early morning through the late day. Strong west to southwest winds developed during the early morning hours across parts of the Black Hills and foothills, when gusts of 60 to over 70 mph were recorded. A strong cold front then moved through from northwest to southeast during the morning and early afternoon. Wind gusts of 60 to over 80 mph developed and persisted through the late day. The strong winds downed some trees and caused isolated power outages in the Black Hills. The winds also helped spread significant wildfires near Mount Rushmore and just west of Rapid City.

A strong winter storm impacted much of western and south central South Dakota, especially the Black Hills area and southwestern South Dakota. Snow lifted slowly north into southern South Dakota during the morning and midday hours, with the most impactful snow near and south of interstate 90. Snow tapered off during the late night and morning hours. Snowfall amounts of six to 12 inches were common across these areas, with one to two feet over parts of the southern Black Hills and far southwestern South Dakota. The heavy, wet snow, combined with wind gusts over 40 mph, produced blizzard or near blizzard conditions across parts of southwestern South Dakota, along with power outages.

Strong west to southwest winds developed during the early morning hours across parts of the Black Hills and foothills, when gusts of 60 to over 70 mph were recorded. A strong cold front then moved through from northwest to southeast during the morning and early afternoon. Wind gusts of 60 to over 80 mph developed and persisted through the late day. The strong winds downed some trees and caused isolated power outages in the Black Hills. The winds also helped spread significant wildfires near Mount Rushmore and just west of Rapid City.

A strong winter storm impacted much of western and south central South Dakota, especially the Black Hills area and southwestern South Dakota. Snow lifted slowly north into southern South Dakota during the morning and midday hours, with the most impactful snow near and south of interstate 90. Snow tapered off during the late night and morning hours. Snowfall amounts of six to 12 inches were common across these areas, with one to two feet over parts of the southern Black Hills and far southwestern South Dakota. The heavy, wet snow, combined with wind gusts over 40 mph, produced blizzard or near blizzard conditions across parts of southwestern South Dakota, along with power outages.

A large slow-moving area of low pressure wandered from Colorado to eastern South Dakota over a two-day period. Precipitation circulated back into southeast South Dakota, which started as a period of rainfall during the morning and early afternoon on March 14.  The rain changed to snow most quickly in south central SD by midday, and slowest toward Interstate 29 which occurred during the evening. Snow became heavy at times during the later evening and overnight, with snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was heavy at times between 2300CST on March 14 and 0400CST on March 15, resulting in very hazardous travel conditions. Totals of 12.7 inches at Brookings, 9.0 inches at Volga and 7.0 inches at White were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the Central Plains, producing areas of heavy snow across southwestern South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell from far southwestern into west central South Dakota, where snowfall was generally four to eight inches. Local amounts around a foot fell in the heaviest band across eastern Oglala Lakota and western Jackson Counties. Lesser amounts fell over the rest of southwestern South Dakota.

Mid-level frontogenesis across southeast South Dakota in the presence of instability aloft allowed for scattered showers to transition into a band of moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the morning which lasted into the evening before tapering off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis across southeast South Dakota in the presence of instability aloft allowed for scattered showers to transition into a band of moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the morning which lasted into the evening before tapering off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility occasionally dropped to less than one mile in snow, which accumulated to 3.0 inches at Chamberlain and 5 miles south of Chamberlain. Roads became snow-covered with dangerous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat and humidity led to strong instability by afternoon, as a weak frontal boundary settled into southeast South Dakota.  Strong instability but weak deep-layer shear resulted in brief, intense and generally outflow-dominant storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds flipped over an irrigation pivot. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite weaker pre-storm deep-layer shear on the order of 25-30 knots and fairly narrow instability profiles of 1000-1500 J/kg, modifications on the storm scale and an increase in the low-level jet improved the environment enough to support several long-lived supercell clusters along a weak frontal boundary. Storms moving through the Sioux Falls area forced an early cancellation of the Sioux Empire Fair.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail produced significant roof damage along a path from south of Dell Rapids to Renner to near Brandon. The most significant damage occurred near Renner where 2 to 3.5 inch hail was reported. Property damage costs are a rough estimate. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota.  An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the rubber roof liner on the section of Lennox High School with the band and shop classrooms was removed by thunderstorm winds. Interior ceilings and equipment incurred water damage. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of Canadian low pressure dropped into the Northern Plains from late Wednesday, January 13th into Friday, January 15th 2021. Snowfall of a trace to four inches combined with high wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph to produce blizzard conditions during the afternoon of the 14th through the morning of the 15th.  Initially, a band of rain moved across the region Wednesday night creating some slick roads as colder air moved in. Many schools were closed or had delayed starts Thursday and Friday.  Due to the slick roads and low visibility, several vehicles ended up in the ditch with I-29 closed from 10pm Thursday into Friday morning from the North Dakota/South Dakota border to Iowa.

A strong northwesterly jet streak digging through the central Rockies produced intense and deep cyclogenesis across Iowa on January 14 and 15.  Initial precipitation swept through the area as rain, but a surge of cold air into the slowing cyclone wrapped snowfall back into areas east of the James River as winds increased to 50 to 70 mph, resulting in areas of blizzard conditions, especially near and east of Interstate 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow redeveloped and began to accumulate toward evening on January 14, the increase in winds to a sustained 30 to 40 mph with a few gusts as high as 62 mph at South Dakota RWIS site 3 miles south of Beresford and 55 mph near North Sioux City, resulted in widespread near white-out conditions.  Interstate 29 was closed in South Dakota at 2200CST January 14 and reopened at 0630CST January 15. Snowfall totaled 2.2 inches 6 miles north northeast of Elk Point. At 1820CST on January 15, a two vehicle accident 2 miles south of Elk Point resulted in the indirect fatality to the driver of a southbound pickup which slid through the median and collided with an oncoming SUV in the northbound lane. Two occupants in the other vehicle suffered indirect injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly jet streak digging through the central Rockies produced intense and deep cyclogenesis across Iowa on January 14 and 15.  Initial precipitation swept through the area as rain, but a surge of cold air into the slowing cyclone wrapped snowfall back into areas east of the James River as winds increased to 50 to 70 mph, resulting in areas of blizzard conditions, especially near and east of Interstate 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow redeveloped toward evening on January 14, the increase in winds to a sustained 30 to 40 mph, with a few gusts as high as 57 mph at South Dakota RWIS site 14 miles west of Ward and 49 mph at SDSU mesonet site at Flandreau, resulted in widespread white-out conditions. Interstate 29 was closed in South Dakota at 2200CST January 14, with the portion north of Sioux Falls reopened by 0730CST January 15. Snowfall totaled 2.0 inches at Flandreau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of Canadian low pressure dropped into the Northern Plains from late Wednesday, January 13th into Friday, January 15th 2021 and brought high winds to central and north central South Dakota. The high winds were responsible for tipping several high profile vehicles over along with causing localized blowing dust. On U.S. Highway 12 near Mobridge, a semi was rolled over with no injuries reported. High wind gusts from 60 to 75 mph occurred across the region. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 70 mph 13 miles northwest of Sansarc, 72 mph 10 miles southwest of Long Lake, and 75 mph 11 miles south of Bullhead.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.6 inches at Brookings and 3.0 inches 2 miles southeast of Astoria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly jet streak digging through the central Rockies produced intense and deep cyclogenesis across Iowa on January 14 and 15.  Initial precipitation swept through the area as rain, but a surge of cold air into the slowing cyclone wrapped snowfall back into areas east of the James River as winds increased to 50 to 70 mph, resulting in areas of blizzard conditions, especially near and east of Interstate 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds at the South Dakota RWIS site near Gayville were occasionally sustained to 40 to 45 mph, with gusts as high as 64 mph around 1831CST on January 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system slowly crossed the region, bringing a prolonged period of very strong northwesterly winds. A tight pressure gradient remained across the Northern Plains, with 36 hours of strong winds over much of the western South Dakota plains. Gusts of 60 to 80 mph were reported across much of the area, with a few higher gusts from Buffalo to the Rapid City area. Some minor damage resulted with isolated power outages and tractor trailers were tipped over on Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly jet streak digging through the central Rockies produced intense and deep cyclogenesis across Iowa on January 14 and 15.  Initial precipitation swept through the area as rain, but a surge of cold air into the slowing cyclone wrapped snowfall back into areas east of the James River as winds increased to 50 to 70 mph, resulting in areas of blizzard conditions, especially near and east of Interstate 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow redeveloped during the late afternoon on January 14, the increase in winds to a sustained 30 to 40 mph, with a few gusts as high as 51 mph at automated site KBKX in Brookings resulted in widespread white-out conditions.  Interstate 29 was closed in South Dakota at 2200CST January 14, with the portion north of Sioux Falls reopened by 0730CST January 15. Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches 2 miles south of Astoria and 2.3 inches 6 miles west of White.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly jet streak digging through the central Rockies produced intense and deep cyclogenesis across Iowa on January 14 and 15.  Initial precipitation swept through the area as rain, but a surge of cold air into the slowing cyclone wrapped snowfall back into areas east of the James River as winds increased to 50 to 70 mph, resulting in areas of blizzard conditions, especially near and east of Interstate 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow redeveloped and began to accumulate toward evening on January 14, the increase in winds to a sustained 30 to 40 mph with a few gusts as high as 55 mph at the South Dakota RWIS site 9 miles east of Davis, resulted in widespread near white-out conditions.  Interstate 29 was closed in South Dakota at 2200CST January 14, and  reopened at 0630CST January 15. Snowfall totaled 2.5 inches on the southern side of Sioux Falls and 2.0 inches at Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of Canadian low pressure dropped into the Northern Plains from late Wednesday, January 13th into Friday, January 15th 2021. Snowfall of a trace to four inches combined with high wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph to produce blizzard conditions during the afternoon of the 14th through the morning of the 15th.  Initially, a band of rain moved across the region Wednesday night creating some slick roads as colder air moved in. Many schools were closed or had delayed starts Thursday and Friday.  Due to the slick roads and low visibility, several vehicles ended up in the ditch with I-29 closed from 10pm Thursday into Friday morning from the North Dakota/South Dakota border to Iowa.

A clipper system moved across the region, bringing snowfall to the northern Black Hills area. A prolonged period of occasional upslope-enhanced snow brought significant accumulations to portions of the northern Black Hills, especially from Lead to Cheyenne Crossing, where eight to 15 inches were reported. Western portions of the central Black Hills received three to six inches of snow over an 18 to 24 hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed west of SD Highway 73, about five miles north of Usta, and was on the ground for about five minutes. No damage was reported as it traveled over open terrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed over Fall River County and tracked northeast across the southern Black Hills into the west central South Dakota plains. A couple of tornadoes developed within the line across Custer County, one south of Custer and the other northeast of Custer. Trees were downed by the tornadoes and the strong straight-line winds that accompanied the line. Hail to quarter size was reported with the storms as well.

Severe to extreme drought conditions continued across north central South Dakota extending towards the James Valley and into parts of central South Dakota. Temperatures were near normal but precipitation was near to around 2 inches below normal across the area. The exception was across portions of Corson and Dewey Counties where locally heavy rain fell, leading to an improvement from Extreme to Severe Conditions from the start to the end of the month. As a result of the continued drought, pasture and range conditions and livestock water sources were rated very poor in quality, and fire danger remained a concern in some areas despite green-up.

An area of low pressure tracked through the Central Plains, and brought heavy snow across portions of central and northeastern South Dakota from the morning of the 10th through the early morning of the 11th. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 8 inches accumulated. Some of the highest amounts included 7 inches at Milbank and in the Watertown area, and 8 inches at Clark and 7 miles WNW of Ames. Road conditions became treacherous and led to several vehicle accidents. One such accident resulted in a fatality in Hamlin County.

A large upper-level low pressure area moved across the Central Plains and produced heavy snow across central South Dakota from the afternoon of the 14th through the morning of the 15th. The heaviest snowfall amounts included 8 inches near Kennebec, Okaton, Pierre and Reliance, and 12 inches at the Chantler Creek Recreation Area. Vehicle accidents resulted from the deteriorated road conditions. Additionally, snow accretion on power lines combined with gusty winds to knock out power to hundreds of customers. Outages lasted into the 16th in some cases.

A strong cold front moved across central and northeastern South Dakota during the afternoon and evening of March 29th, bringing high winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts of 60 to over 70 mph into the early morning on the 30th. These winds lofted dust across the area, reducing visibility in some cases, and blew over a semi-trailer in Grant County. Also, a large, wind-driven fire occurred in Jones County. Some of the strongest recorded wind gusts were 72 mph 11 miles S of Bullhead, at Cravens Corner, 2 miles ENE of Sisseton, and 6 miles SSW of Peever.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions, which began in late February, continued through March and became extreme by the end of March across north central South Dakota. The month on the whole was well above average temperature-wise, and precipitation was near average to well below average across central and north central South Dakota. Mobridge recorded their 4th driest March on record. Soil moisture and stock ponds continued to be depleted. The Grassland Fire Danger reached high to extreme levels several times throughout the month, with grassland fires reported as well.

A severe thunderstorm developed over southeastern Perkins County and moved east-southeast into Ziebach County. The storm produced hail and wind gusts to 80 mph.

A line of thunderstorms developed over far northeastern Wyoming and became severe as it moved into western South Dakota. The line remained together through much of northwestern and west central South Dakota. Hail accompanied the stronger cells within the line, but strong wind gusts were the main threats with the storms.

A fire ignited about 6 miles northwest of Okaton in Jones County during the afternoon on March 29th. Critical fire weather conditions were in place across the region given the very low relative humidity and strong winds. Though the fire initially spread east-northeast due to westerly winds, a passing cold front shifted winds to the northwest and thus the fire's propagation to the southeast. A dramatic increase in winds with gusts to around 60 mph during this time led to the development of a large, wind-driven fire which eventually reached and passed over Interstate-90. The interstate was closed from 3 to 6 pm as a result. A total of around 9,500 acres of grassland were burned, per the Great Plains Fire Information Center. Over 100 customers lost power during the fire and high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts at near 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm became severe over southern Meade County and tracked southeast through portions of eastern Pennington and western Jackson Counties before weakening. The storm produced large hail around golf ball size and wind gusts to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size covered the ground.

Severe to extreme drought conditions continued from June into July across most of the area. Extreme Drought/D3 expanded some across portions of central and into northeast South Dakota throughout the month. Beneficial rainfall during the second week of the month brought improved drought conditions in the Glacial Lakes area of northeast South Dakota. For July, the driest places were from north of Pierre, northeastward through Aberdeen, and over to Marshall and Day counties. This area was generally 1 to 2 inches below normal.  Above-average temperatures were observed across South Dakota during July, with several days in the 90s to the lower 100s. The Moreau River near Whitehorse and Bois De Sioux River near White Rock dropped to the 10th percentile with the warm and dry conditions. The drought conditions significantly impacted crops. Spring wheat closely resembled conditions during the 1988 drought. A shortage of pastureland and feed caused a growing concern for the lack of feed for the winter months. Many cattle producers were forced to sell off some of their cattle.||Stock ponds and creeks remained very low or completely dry. Drought conditions contributed to several grassland fires across central and northeast South Dakota throughout July as well. State parks in the Pierre area issued burn bans. Historically dry fuels were observed in central and north central South Dakota, with ERC/Energy Release Components above the 90th percentile (ERC-measure of the fuel moisture related to potential fire intensity).

A wildfire was started 15 miles northwest of Fort Pierre by a combine. The fire consumed about 307 acres. Gusty northerly winds of 20 to 25 mph and low relative humidity of 9 to 13 percent were big factors in the fire spread.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over Butte County and tracked southeast across portions of western South Dakota into Nebraska before weakening. The storm produced very large hail to baseball size along its path. The storm passed directly over the NWS Rapid City Doppler radar near New Underwood and tennis ball sized hail caused significant damage to the radar dome.

Low visibility from heavy rain and fog contributed to the collision of two boats on Indian Springs Lake, southeast of Clark, resulting in one fatality and four injuries.

Thunderstorms developed near an area of low pressure across central South Dakota during the afternoon of July 25th, quickly intensifying to severe limits. These storms produced, at times, large to significantly large hail as they tracked to the east and southeast. The hail caused structural, vehicle, and crop damage. One of the more prolific storms produced hail continuously over a 3-hour window of time, extending about 125 miles from Faulk to Codington counties. The hail was up to nearly 4 inches in diameter at one location along the way.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail to tennis ball size caused significant damage to the dome of the NWS Rapid City Doppler radar (KUDX).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions continued from June into July across most of the area. Extreme Drought/D3 expanded some across portions of central and into northeast South Dakota throughout the month. Beneficial rainfall during the second week of the month brought improved drought conditions in the Glacial Lakes area of northeast South Dakota. For July, the driest places were from north of Pierre, northeastward through Aberdeen, and over to Marshall and Day counties. This area was generally 1 to 2 inches below normal.  Above-average temperatures were observed across South Dakota during July, with several days in the 90s to the lower 100s. The Moreau River near Whitehorse and Bois De Sioux River near White Rock dropped to the 10th percentile with the warm and dry conditions. The drought conditions significantly impacted crops. Spring wheat closely resembled conditions during the 1988 drought. A shortage of pastureland and feed caused a growing concern for the lack of feed for the winter months. Many cattle producers were forced to sell off some of their cattle.||Stock ponds and creeks remained very low or completely dry. Drought conditions contributed to several grassland fires across central and northeast South Dakota throughout July as well. State parks in the Pierre area issued burn bans. Historically dry fuels were observed in central and north central South Dakota, with ERC/Energy Release Components above the 90th percentile (ERC-measure of the fuel moisture related to potential fire intensity).

Thunderstorms developed near an area of low pressure across central South Dakota during the afternoon of July 25th, quickly intensifying to severe limits. These storms produced, at times, large to significantly large hail as they tracked to the east and southeast. The hail caused structural, vehicle, and crop damage. One of the more prolific storms produced hail continuously over a 3-hour window of time, extending about 125 miles from Faulk to Codington counties. The hail was up to nearly 4 inches in diameter at one location along the way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions continued from June into July across most of the area. Extreme Drought/D3 expanded some across portions of central and into northeast South Dakota throughout the month. Beneficial rainfall during the second week of the month brought improved drought conditions in the Glacial Lakes area of northeast South Dakota. For July, the driest places were from north of Pierre, northeastward through Aberdeen, and over to Marshall and Day counties. This area was generally 1 to 2 inches below normal.  Above-average temperatures were observed across South Dakota during July, with several days in the 90s to the lower 100s. The Moreau River near Whitehorse and Bois De Sioux River near White Rock dropped to the 10th percentile with the warm and dry conditions. The drought conditions significantly impacted crops. Spring wheat closely resembled conditions during the 1988 drought. A shortage of pastureland and feed caused a growing concern for the lack of feed for the winter months. Many cattle producers were forced to sell off some of their cattle.||Stock ponds and creeks remained very low or completely dry. Drought conditions contributed to several grassland fires across central and northeast South Dakota throughout July as well. State parks in the Pierre area issued burn bans. Historically dry fuels were observed in central and north central South Dakota, with ERC/Energy Release Components above the 90th percentile (ERC-measure of the fuel moisture related to potential fire intensity).

Severe to extreme drought conditions continued leading into August with a slight expansion of D3 (Extreme) early in the month.  Severe to extreme drought conditions covered roughly 80 percent of the region. High to very high fire danger was noted frequently across central and north central South Dakota and fires continued to be an issue with widespread dry fuels for the first half of the month. Agriculturally, these conditions mimic those of the drought in 1988, when all central and northeast South Dakota counties were assigned a drought disaster designation. Several rounds of storms and a widespread stratiform rain event during the latter half of the month brought much needed moisture. Some locations surpassed 200 percent of normal rainfall for August, including Sisseton which recorded a new monthly record. This moisture and closer to average temperatures resulted in a slight diminishment of drought conditions, with surviving cool season grasses coming out of dormancy. The area that received the greatest improvement was the northeast part of South Dakota.

Slow moving thunderstorms formed in the James Valley and across north central South Dakota. The storms just north of Aberdeen brought multiple reports of large hail and severe winds. The strongest storm moved along a boundary to the east of Aberdeen between the evening and early overnight hours, before storms became more scattered and sub-severe.

A cluster of storms eventually merged into one strong cell east of the Aberdeen area during the afternoon with numerous large hail and damaging wind reports. Hail topped out at baseball diameter and winds peaked at 90 mph. The system also produced a tornado before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief touchdown of a tornado occurred with no damage reported.

The area that received the greatest improvement was the northeast part of South Dakota.

Severe to extreme drought conditions continued leading into August with a slight expansion of D3 (Extreme) early in the month.  Severe to extreme drought conditions covered roughly 80 percent of the region. High to very high fire danger was noted frequently across central and north central South Dakota and fires continued to be an issue with widespread dry fuels for the first half of the month. Agriculturally, these conditions mimic those of the drought in 1988, when all central and northeast South Dakota counties were assigned a drought disaster designation. Several rounds of storms and a widespread stratiform rain event during the latter half of the month brought much needed moisture. Low pressure intensified on December 10 as it moved from the lee of the central Rockies into the Great Lakes.  A strong frontogenetic band developed ahead of the system, with weak instability present which resulted in a narrow and more intense band across parts of southeast South Dakota.  Snowfall from 6 to 11 inches was common along the axis of the band from south central South Dakota toward the Sioux Falls area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was moderate to heavy at times between 1100-1700CST, and produced dangerous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 6.2 inches at Tyndall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system raced northeast from Colorado to southern Minnesota from the morning to the evening of December 15th. Strong to high wind gusts resulted across the area from the afternoon on the 15th into the early morning of the 16th, the strongest of which included 65 mph 2 miles NE of Mina, 65 mph 3 miles SE of Reliance, and 64 mph 1 mile NNW of Lees Corner. Additionally, this system produced dense fog, snow of up to 2, blowing snow, freezing rain, and a flash freeze from rapidly dropping temperatures following evening rain across northeastern South Dakota. Travel was adversely impacted. ||Further southeast, this system produced a historic and unprecedented severe weather outbreak across Nebraska, Iowa, Minnesota, and Wisconsin, with record-setting numbers of tornadoes and significant thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system deepened while it tracked from the Dakotas on the 10th into Minnesota and Wisconsin by the morning of the 11th, and then further deepened as it stalled through the 12th. This system generated high wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph across central, north central and northeast South Dakota on the 11th and 12th; the strongest measured gust was 70 mph at Hayes, SD. Several high-profile vehicles were tipped as a result, with at least 10 between Kadoka and Murdo on Interstate 90.

An unprecedented storm system lifted across the central Plains from December 15 into the early hours of December 16. Kinematic fields were at the top of the climatological distributions, leading to extreme wind shear in the lowest levels. Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes.||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force. Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 50 mph during the late evening, with a peak wind gust of 59 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System near Tripp (station SD570).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system raced northeast from Colorado to southern Minnesota from the morning to the evening of December 15th. Strong to high wind gusts resulted across the area from the afternoon on the 15th into the early morning of the 16th, the strongest of which included 65 mph 2 miles NE of Mina, 65 mph 3 miles SE of Reliance, and 64 mph 1 mile NNW of Lees Corner. Additionally, this system produced dense fog, snow of up to 2, blowing snow, freezing rain, and a flash freeze from rapidly dropping temperatures following evening rain across northeastern South Dakota. Travel was adversely impacted. ||Further southeast, this system produced a historic and unprecedented severe weather outbreak across Nebraska, Iowa, Minnesota, and Wisconsin, with record-setting numbers of tornadoes and significant thunderstorm wind gusts.

Late October precipitation eased drought conditions across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between one and one and one-half inches of precipitation during the final few days of October at Montrose and 5 miles northeast of Salem led to improvement in the drought conditions from severe (D2) to moderate (D1). Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system deepened while it tracked from the Dakotas on the 10th into Minnesota and Wisconsin by the morning of the 11th, and then further deepened as it stalled through the 12th. This system generated high wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph across central, north central and northeast South Dakota on the 11th and 12th; the strongest measured gust was 70 mph at Hayes, SD. Several high-profile vehicles were tipped as a result, with at least 10 between Kadoka and Murdo on Interstate 90.

Surface low pressure stalled across far eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin for much of November 11 into November 12.  A strong synoptic pressure gradient intensified over eastern South Dakota, with several locations experiencing numerous wind gusts over 58 mph, and many sustained northwest winds around 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During several hours which featured sustained winds of 30 to 38 mph with wind gusts of 50 to 55 mph, a wind gust of 58 mph was measured by the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Plankinton (SD553).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure stalled across far eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin for much of November 11 into November 12.  A strong synoptic pressure gradient intensified over eastern South Dakota, with several locations experiencing numerous wind gusts over 58 mph, and many sustained northwest winds around 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds periodically reached 30 to 40 mph, with the RAWS site near Geddes (RLAS2) measuring a peak wind gust of 58 mph and the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site at Tripp (SD570) measuring a peak wind gust of 56 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-mixed thermodynamic profile to the southwest of the track of an intensifying area of low pressure across central Minnesota led to a brief pulse of very strong winds behind a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 71 mph was measured by the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site at the Fort Randall Dam (SD588).

A low pressure system deepened while it tracked from the Dakotas on the 10th into Minnesota and Wisconsin by the morning of the 11th, and then further deepened as it stalled through the 12th. This system brought the first accumulating snow of the season, of a dusting to locally 5 inches in Webster, as well as wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph across northeastern South Dakota. Blizzard conditions were contingent on when and where snow was falling from the 11th into the 12th, mainly focused across the Sisseton Hills.

Low pressure intensified on December 10 as it moved from the lee of the central Rockies into the Great Lakes.  A strong frontogenetic band developed ahead of the system, with weak instability present which resulted in a narrow and more intense band across parts of southeast South Dakota.  Snowfall from 6 to 11 inches was common along the axis of the band from south central South Dakota toward the Sioux Falls area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was moderate to heavy at times between 0500-1400CST, and produced treacherous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 11.0 inches near Academy and 9.0 inches at Platte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure stalled across far eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin for much of November 11 into November 12.  A strong synoptic pressure gradient intensified over eastern South Dakota, with several locations experiencing numerous wind gusts over 58 mph, and many sustained northwest winds around 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph occurred at both the RAWS site 10 miles south-southwest of Wessington (RHUS2) and the South Dakota Road Weather Information System sites near Plankinton (SD553) and Gann Valley (SD531), the latter each measuring a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds periodically reached 40 to 45 mph, with the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Ward (SD577) measuring a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unprecedented storm system lifted across the central Plains from December 15 into the early hours of December 16. Kinematic fields were at the top of the climatological distributions, leading to extreme wind shear in the lowest levels. Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes.||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force. Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 45 mph during the evening, with a peak wind gust of 64 mph at the Madison Municipal Airport (KMDS). In addition, snowfall resulted in brief visibility restrictions below 1 mile, making travel difficult.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 60 mph during the evening and into the overnight, with a peak wind gust of 74 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System at Fort Randall Dam (station SD588).

Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes.||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force. Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 59 mph was recorded at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System near Howard (station SD597). Sustained winds reached 40 to 50 mph at Mitchell (station KMHE) during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system raced northeast from Colorado to southern Minnesota from the morning to the evening of December 15th. Strong to high wind gusts resulted across the area from the afternoon on the 15th into the early morning of the 16th, the strongest of which included 65 mph 2 miles NE of Mina, 65 mph 3 miles SE of Reliance, and 64 mph 1 mile NNW of Lees Corner. Additionally, this system produced dense fog, snow of up to 2, blowing snow, freezing rain, and a flash freeze from rapidly dropping temperatures following evening rain across northeastern South Dakota. Travel was adversely impacted. ||Further southeast, this system produced a historic and unprecedented severe weather outbreak across Nebraska, Iowa, Minnesota, and Wisconsin, with record-setting numbers of tornadoes and significant thunderstorm wind gusts.

A low pressure system deepened while it tracked from the Dakotas on the 10th into Minnesota and Wisconsin by the morning of the 11th, and then further deepened as it stalled through the 12th. This system generated high wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph across central, north central and northeast South Dakota on the 11th and 12th; the strongest measured gust was 70 mph at Hayes, SD. Several high-profile vehicles were tipped as a result, with at least 10 between Kadoka and Murdo on Interstate 90.

An unprecedented storm system lifted across the central Plains from December 15 into the early hours of December 16. Kinematic fields were at the top of the climatological distributions, leading to extreme wind shear in the lowest levels. Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes.||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force. Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached around 40 mph during the evening at a site 13 miles south southwest of Wessington (station RHUS2).

A cluster of severe storms developed in northwestern South Dakota during the late afternoon hours and migrated east, crossing north central and northeast South Dakota through the overnight and early morning hours. This was primarily a wind producing complex with gusts in excess of 100 mph at times which caused sporadic damage.

Severe thunderstorms developed across north central South Dakota around midnight and proceeded east through the overnight hours. Of particular note, a long-track supercell produced severe weather from just east of the Missouri River in Walworth County along the length of Highway 12 through the rest of the state, a distance of about 180 miles. Additional severe thunderstorms developed across the area during and after this long-track supercell dissipated, during the mid-morning. The strongest storms produced tennis to baseball sized hail and damaging wind gusts.||The hail was large and numerous enough to produce significant and virtually continuous crop damage for about 90 miles from west of Pembrook Colony in Edmunds County east to about Andover in Day County, as seen from satellite as a hail scar. This scar was about 6 miles wide in spots. The crop damage was more sporadic afterwards, though the hail scar was still discernable south of Waubay in Day County east to the north of Milbank in Grant County. Along with the crop damage, many buildings, windows, and vehicles were damaged along the storm's path. The large hail also killed deer and many birds.

Severe thunderstorms developed across north central South Dakota around midnight and proceeded east through the overnight hours. Of particular note, a long-track supercell produced severe weather from just east of the Missouri River in Walworth County along the length of Highway 12 through the rest of the state, a distance of about 180 miles. Additional severe thunderstorms developed across the area during and after this long-track supercell dissipated, during the mid-morning. The strongest storms produced tennis to baseball sized hail and damaging wind gusts.||The hail was large and numerous enough to produce significant and virtually continuous crop damage for about 90 miles from west of Pembrook Colony in Edmunds County east to about Andover in Day County, as seen from satellite as a hail scar. This scar was about 6 miles wide in spots. The crop damage was more sporadic afterwards, though the hail scar was still discernable south of Waubay in Day County east to the north of Milbank in Grant County. Along with the crop damage, many buildings, windows, and vehicles were damaged along the storm's path. The large hail also killed deer and many birds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hail swath determined by high resolution satellite imagery along with surface reports occurred from Edmunds County east across southern Brown into Day Counties.  The largest hail size was baseball size. The scar dimensions covered approximately 70 square miles in Edmunds County. The start location was approximately 5 miles southwest of Ipswich, and it continued in an east southeast orientation to the Brown County line approximately 5 miles south of Mina Lake. Drone and photo evidence of damage in this area suggest substantial to total crop losses.

High wake low winds of up to over 60 mph then developed on the backside of the system. Trees, branches, power lines and poles were downed, and some structural damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wake low high winds of 58 mph were recorded at Waubay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe storms developed in northwestern South Dakota during the late afternoon hours and migrated east, crossing north central and northeast South Dakota through the overnight and early morning hours. This was primarily a wind producing complex with gusts in excess of 100 mph at times which caused sporadic damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 100 mph winds took shingles off a home and tin off an outbuilding along with downing many large tree branches.  A nearby mesonet station recorded a wind gust of over 100 mph.

Severe thunderstorms developed across north central South Dakota around midnight and proceeded east through the overnight hours. Of particular note, a long-track supercell produced severe weather from just east of the Missouri River in Walworth County along the length of Highway 12 through the rest of the state, a distance of about 180 miles. Additional severe thunderstorms developed across the area during and after this long-track supercell dissipated, during the mid-morning. The strongest storms produced tennis to baseball sized hail and damaging wind gusts.||The hail was large and numerous enough to produce significant and virtually continuous crop damage for about 90 miles from west of Pembrook Colony in Edmunds County east to about Andover in Day County, as seen from satellite as a hail scar. This scar was about 6 miles wide in spots. The crop damage was more sporadic afterwards, though the hail scar was still discernable south of Waubay in Day County east to the north of Milbank in Grant County. Along with the crop damage, many buildings, windows, and vehicles were damaged along the storm's path. The large hail also killed deer and many birds.

An area of rain with embedded isolated thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a low pressure area and a stationary front across western South Dakota on the evening of Sunday, September 19th. One storm produced a couple reports of severe-level hail and wind across north central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar hailstones lasted a couple minutes and produced minor damage to a home.

A strong cold front swept across central and northeastern South Dakota Sunday night, September 19th, into Monday morning, September 20th, and produced wind gusts from the north/northwest in excess of 58 mph. The strongest gusts include 63 mph eleven miles south of Bullhead, 61 mph three miles north of Agar, and 60 mph at the Pierre Municipal Airport.

Widespread drought conditions, which began in February, continued into and through September. Much of Central South Dakota benefited from above average rainfall and thus a slight improvement in drought, thanks in large part to a widespread heavy rain event on the 2nd. However, severe to extreme drought persisted across north central South Dakota, as slightly below average precipitation fell. Temperatures were between 1 and 4 degrees above normal for the month on the whole. Around the start of the month, 72% of the NWS Aberdeen's forecast area was in D2/D3 (31% in just D3), but that percentage dropped to 57% (5%) by the end of the month.||As of the middle of the month, drought continued to adversely impact agriculture and hydrology. The National Agriculture Statistics Service reported a modest improvement in topsoil and subsoil moisture across South Dakota, while corn, soybean and pasture & range conditions were rated 44%, 41% and 80% poor to very poor, respectively. Additionally, increased whitetail deer kill had been observed across Central South Dakota. This is due to an increase in midge flies, which carry disease, as there's been greater areas of mud flat exposure for breeding.

An intense upper level system moved from the central Rockies to the northern Plains, bringing heavy precipitation to the area. Temperatures were cold enough for mostly or all snow across the Black Hills. Snowfall amounts of one to two feet fell over the northern Black Hills, with lesser amounts across the rest of the Black Hills. Rain changed to snow over the higher elevations of Butte and Harding Counties, with as much as six to 12 inches in some areas.

Rain changed to snow over the higher elevations of Butte and Harding Counties, with as much as six to 12 inches in some areas.

During the afternoon hours, a line of storms along a boundary moved east-northeast across northeastern South Dakota. The storms produced locally heavy rainfall, straight-line winds of 60 to 75 mph, and three tornadoes. The tornadoes were only briefly on the ground and caused EF-0 damage. More tornado warnings were issued by NWS Aberdeen on this one day, 12, than any previous monthly total in August, September, or October since at least 1986 when record keeping began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department reported a brief tornado touchdown.

Severe (D2) drought was confined to eastern Dewey, Stanley, northwestern Jones, extreme northwest Lyman, western Hughes, western Potter, and western Sully counties.||During the middle portion of October, stream flows were normal to much above average on area rivers and streams. Even central South Dakota, the Moreau, and Grand rivers ran above average. Pasture and range conditions were rated 44 percent very poor and 35 percent poor. The grassland fire danger was high to very high at times across the area, mainly during low humidity and high winds.

An Arctic airmass settled in across the Northern Plains from the evening of December 28th through the 31st and into the new year (see the January 2022 Storm Data entry for additional details). Wind chill warning criteria was met from the 28th into the 29th under dominant high pressure, and again during the morning and evening of the 31st when extreme cold air was reinforced by a passing cold front, across at least portions of north central and northeastern South Dakota. Only slight improvements in conditions were noted during the daytime and between these waves. Some of the coldest wind chill readings during this period include -47 degrees F 4 miles SW of Hillhead, -44 F 4 miles NNW of Victor, -43 F 3 miles south of Webster, and -42 F 7 miles south of Claremont, 2 miles north of Herreid, 6 miles west of Henry, and 3 miles WNW of Wallace.

A complex of thunderstorms that began on the evening of June 7th across southeastern North Dakota back-built to the west through the night and then to the southwest through the day on June 8th. This complex impacted north central South Dakota by the early evening on the 8th, leading to flash flooding, severe hail and wind, and a tornado. A favorable environment enabled these storms to produce measured wind gusts generally of 60 to 70 mph, though one report near Bullhead exceeded 90 mph. Rainfall amounts across central Corson County ranged from 3 to over 10 inches.

Severe to extreme drought conditions continued from May into June, and expanded across the area through the month of June due to much above average temperatures and much below average rainfall. Statewide, South Dakota recorded its 4th warmest and its driest June since record keeping began in 1895. By the end of the month, D3/Extreme Drought was affecting much of north central South Dakota as well as portions of Lyman County, and D2/Severe Drought was affecting at least portions of all but Deuel, Grant and Codington Counties. Impacts from the ongoing and worsening drought include below to much below normal stream flows, entirely dry or very low stock ponds, creeks and marshes, and fire danger increased due to fuels that had begun to be or had already completely cured. Additionally, crop and pasture and range conditions had been rated poor to very poor across the board, grasshoppers had become a big concern around Eagle Butte, many wheat fields had been cut for silage, and cattle producers had been strained as well. South Dakota governor declared a statewide state of emergency for drought conditions, and the USDA designated several counites as primary natural disaster areas.

An area of low pressure was positioned across southern South Dakota for much of the day on the 17th, and this allowed for a band of heavy snow to develop and stall across portions of northeastern South Dakota. Heavy snow of locally over a foot accumulated during this time, and travel was significantly impacted. Some of the highest amounts include 14 inches 2 miles SSW of Havana, 13 inches in Kidder, 12 inches 5 miles east of Britton, 11 inches 7 miles WNW of Hecla, 10 inches at Roy Lake State Park, 4 miles west of Claire City, and Britton, and 9.8 inches in Sisseton.

A surface low pressure system deepened as it tracked across the Northern Plains. Generally 1-2 of snow fell across north central and northeastern South Dakota on the evening of the 4th into the morning of the 5th, and strong to high winds followed throughout the day on the 5th. Some of the strongest gusts occurred across central South Dakota and across the Sisseton Hills: 61 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport and 3 miles north of Altamont, and 60 mph 1 mile NNW of Lees Corner and 1 mile WNW of Summit.
