Severe to extreme drought conditions continued from May into June, and expanded across the area through the month of June due to much above average temperatures and much below average rainfall. Statewide, South Dakota recorded its 4th warmest and its driest June since record keeping began in 1895. By the end of the month, D3/Extreme Drought was affecting much of north central South Dakota as well as portions of Lyman County, and D2/Severe Drought was affecting at least portions of all but Deuel, Grant and Codington Counties. Impacts from the ongoing and worsening drought include below to much below normal stream flows, entirely dry or very low stock ponds, creeks and marshes, and fire danger increased due to fuels that had begun to be or had already completely cured. Additionally, crop and pasture and range conditions had been rated poor to very poor across the board, grasshoppers had become a big concern around Eagle Butte, many wheat fields had been cut for silage, and cattle producers had been strained as well. South Dakota governor declared a statewide state of emergency for drought conditions, and the USDA designated several counites as primary natural disaster areas.

An area of low pressure tracked and deepened across South Dakota from December 26th into the 27th. Gusty winds, freezing drizzle, heavy snow, blowing snow, and even thundersnow resulted across portions of central and/or northeastern South Dakota. Travel was adversely impacted, with several accidents reported. The highest snowfall totals include 6 inches in Milbank and 8 miles ENE of Sisseton, 5.8 inches in La Bolt, 5.5 inches 1 mile west of Twin Brooks, 5.3 inches 4 miles west of Sisseton, and 5 inches in Clear Lake and 7 miles WNW of Hecla.

An Arctic airmass settled in across the Northern Plains from the evening of December 28th through the 31st and into the new year (see the January 2022 Storm Data entry for additional details). Wind chill warning criteria was met from the 28th into the 29th under dominant high pressure, and again during the morning and evening of the 31st when extreme cold air was reinforced by a passing cold front, across at least portions of north central and northeastern South Dakota. Only slight improvements in conditions were noted during the daytime and between these waves. Some of the coldest wind chill readings during this period include -47 degrees F 4 miles SW of Hillhead, -44 F 4 miles NNW of Victor, -43 F 3 miles south of Webster, and -42 F 7 miles south of Claremont, 2 miles north of Herreid, 6 miles west of Henry, and 3 miles WNW of Wallace.

Wind chill warning criteria was met from the 28th into the 29th under dominant high pressure, and again during the morning and evening of the 31st when extreme cold air was reinforced by a passing cold front, across at least portions of north central and northeastern South Dakota. Only slight improvements in conditions were noted during the daytime and between these waves. Some of the coldest wind chill readings during this period include -47 degrees F 4 miles SW of Hillhead, -44 F 4 miles NNW of Victor, -43 F 3 miles south of Webster, and -42 F 7 miles south of Claremont, 2 miles north of Herreid, 6 miles west of Henry, and 3 miles WNW of Wallace.

Severe to extreme drought conditions continued from May into June, and expanded across the area through the month of June due to much above average temperatures and much below average rainfall. Statewide, South Dakota recorded its 4th warmest and its driest June since record keeping began in 1895. By the end of the month, D3/Extreme Drought was affecting much of north central South Dakota as well as portions of Lyman County, and D2/Severe Drought was affecting at least portions of all but Deuel, Grant and Codington Counties. Impacts from the ongoing and worsening drought include below to much below normal stream flows, entirely dry or very low stock ponds, creeks and marshes, and fire danger increased due to fuels that had begun to be or had already completely cured. Additionally, crop and pasture and range conditions had been rated poor to very poor across the board, grasshoppers had become a big concern around Eagle Butte, many wheat fields had been cut for silage, and cattle producers had been strained as well. South Dakota governor declared a statewide state of emergency for drought conditions, and the USDA designated several counites as primary natural disaster areas.

An unprecedented storm system lifted across the central Plains from December 15 into the early hours of December 16. Kinematic fields were at the top of the climatological distributions, leading to extreme wind shear in the lowest levels. Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes.||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force. Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 45 mph during the evening and into the overnight, with a peak wind gust of 60 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System near Arlington (station SD504).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong impulse traversed the periphery of a strong ridge aloft over the central Plains, scattered thunderstorms developed during the early evening into far southern South Dakota.  With instability of around 1500 J/kg and a large part within favorable hail formation temperatures, strong updrafts developed quickly with several reports of large hail.  The inverted-V type profiles also led to spotty damaging wind as storms weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds were measured by a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site at Fort Randall Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms which developed across western South Dakota during the evening of June 10 translated eastward through the overnight hours.  An increasing low-level jet enhanced shear, with an environment strongly supportive of developing outflow pools with DCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg.  Storms congealed into a linear bowing structure through the late overnight and early morning hours, producing numerous instances of strong to damaging wind gusts.  One (direct) injury occurred in Yankton when a tree fell on an elderly man at a senior housing complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of a large tree was blown down by strong thunderstorm winds. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions and dry fuels, combined with low humidity and gusty southerly wind allowed an accidental fire to quickly grow and scorch over 150 acres of forest and grassland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire started behind a business just north of Pickstown along U.S. Highway 18, and spread northward due to a southerly wind which gusted to 30 to 35 mph.  The fire spread quickly into tree-covered hillsides which forced the evacuation of several residences, a campground, and a swimming beach. The swimming beach was put on lockdown after an initial group was evacuated as the fire threatened the safety of 83 additional evacuees.  The fire caused sparking along powerlines which started another 50-acre fire about three miles from the earlier ignition point.  The fire caused a power, water, and cellular service outage for the Pickstown area for up to three hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several elevated supercells developed in a high shear/moderate CAPE environment as the low-level jet increased during the late evening and early overnight hours.  Large hail was the primary outcome from these storms.

Heat and humidity led to strong instability by afternoon, as a weak frontal boundary settled into southeast South Dakota.  Strong instability but weak deep-layer shear resulted in brief, intense and generally outflow-dominant storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous 3 to 6 inch diameter limbs were downed by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite weaker pre-storm deep-layer shear on the order of 25-30 knots and fairly narrow instability profiles of 1000-1500 J/kg, modifications on the storm scale and an increase in the low-level jet improved the environment enough to support several long-lived supercell clusters along a weak frontal boundary. Storms moving through the Sioux Falls area forced an early cancellation of the Sioux Empire Fair.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several large tree limbs at the Mary Jo Wegner Arboretum, some as large as one foot in diameter. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

An elevated supercell storm developed along a mid-level frontal boundary as a weak wave helped to increase and focus the low-level jet. With fairly weak low-level stability, severe winds easily reached the surface along the track of the storm as it transitioned to a linear structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees in the Lake Sinai area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota.  An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was measured by a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall, precipitation near to a little above normal across southeast South Dakota led to the alleviation of severe drought for some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures at White Lake were 2 to 3 degrees above normal during September with precipitation from 50 to 75 percent of normal, which led to a continuation of extreme (D3) drought conditions. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite temperatures from 3 to 4 degrees above normal at Huron during September, 125-150 percent of normal precipitation allowed extreme (D3) drought conditions to improve to severe (D2) drought at mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal and precipitation mainly below normal during the month. Some improvement in drought conditions were observed by month's end across portions of Minnehaha and Lincoln Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought eased to moderate (D1) drought after an early month inch of rainfall.  After a two-week dry period mid-month, severe drought conditions returned late month, only to again improve after 3 to 4 inches of rainfall between August 21 and August 29. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow zone of enhanced instability developed ahead of a cold front with up to 1500-2000 J/kg MLCAPE.  As a mid-level jet streak crossed the boundary in the late afternoon and evening, deep layer shear increased to around 50 knots. Isolated thunderstorms developed supercell characteristics, resulting in large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a response to 3 to 5 inches of rainfall across the southern portions of Sioux Falls, the Big Sioux at Western Avenue (gage site WAVS2) climbed into flood stage at 2130CST, with a crest 0.94 feet above flood stage at 2230CST.  The Sioux Falls Marathon, which was scheduled to begin at 0530CST, was cancelled due to the overnight flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite weaker pre-storm deep-layer shear on the order of 25-30 knots and fairly narrow instability profiles of 1000-1500 J/kg, modifications on the storm scale and an increase in the low-level jet improved the environment enough to support several long-lived supercell clusters along a weak frontal boundary. Storms moving through the Sioux Falls area forced an early cancellation of the Sioux Empire Fair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat and humidity led to strong instability by afternoon, as a weak frontal boundary settled into southeast South Dakota.  Strong instability but weak deep-layer shear resulted in brief, intense and generally outflow-dominant storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large portion of the rubber roofing was damaged at the Expo Building on the W.H. Lyon Fairgrounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms which developed across western South Dakota during the evening of June 10 translated eastward through the overnight hours.  An increasing low-level jet enhanced shear, with an environment strongly supportive of developing outflow pools with DCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg.  Storms congealed into a linear bowing structure through the late overnight and early morning hours, producing numerous instances of strong to damaging wind gusts.  One (direct) injury occurred in Yankton when a tree fell on an elderly man at a senior housing complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 65 mph damaged a portion of the roof on a farm shed, as well as damaged siding on a home. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed late in the day north of a warm frontal boundary across far northern Nebraska.  With elevated instability around 2000 J/kg and deep layer effective shear of 40 knots, storms took on a brief supercell structure and favored large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary.  With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were measured by a personal weather station 2 miles north northeast of Burbank. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several elevated supercells developed in a high shear/moderate CAPE environment as the low-level jet increased during the late evening and early overnight hours.  Large hail was the primary outcome from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal and precipitation mainly below normal during the month. Some improvement in drought conditions were observed by month's end across portions of Minnehaha and Lincoln Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought continued through August, with less than one-third to one-half inch of precipitation until the very end of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid degradation in drought status occurred in June as temperatures averaged 5 to 6 degrees above normal and precipitation varied between 25 and 50 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed early in the month. After a few more weeks of lack of precipitation and extreme heat (0.84 inches/11 days of 10 plus degrees above normal at Gregory), conditions worsened to extreme (D3) drought. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota.  An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up 4 miles northeast of Worthing, one-half mile north of 278th Street just east of 475th Avenue. The tornado tracked northeast for a little over a mile before it lifted 4 miles south of Harrisburg, just southwest of the intersection of 277th Street and 476th Avenue.  Damage was limited to crops with the path defined on Sentinel satellite imagery. The average path width was 20 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms which developed across western South Dakota during the evening of June 10 translated eastward through the overnight hours.  An increasing low-level jet enhanced shear, with an environment strongly supportive of developing outflow pools with DCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg.  Storms congealed into a linear bowing structure through the late overnight and early morning hours, producing numerous instances of strong to damaging wind gusts.  One (direct) injury occurred in Yankton when a tree fell on an elderly man at a senior housing complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather station KMHE measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near to around two degrees above normal during the month, while precipitation was spotty, and generally varied from 50 to 100 percent of normal for most areas. Parts of far southeast South Dakota received near 150 percent of normal precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought moderated after mid-month to severe (D2) drought, courtesy of a period of below normal temperatures as well as over two inches of rainfall at Tyndall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite weaker pre-storm deep-layer shear on the order of 25-30 knots and fairly narrow instability profiles of 1000-1500 J/kg, modifications on the storm scale and an increase in the low-level jet improved the environment enough to support several long-lived supercell clusters along a weak frontal boundary. Storms moving through the Sioux Falls area forced an early cancellation of the Sioux Empire Fair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat and humidity led to strong instability by afternoon, as a weak frontal boundary settled into southeast South Dakota.  Strong instability but weak deep-layer shear resulted in brief, intense and generally outflow-dominant storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was pea-sized, with a few stones as large as quarters. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary.  With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust was measured by the South Dakota State Mesonet site 2 miles northeast of Brookings (BKMS2). Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small mesoscale convective cluster of storms developed in an area of steep mid-level lapse rates and marginal deep-layer shear. The northern edge of the cluster brushed into far southern South Dakota, with an isolated incident of large hail near the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported with occasional ping-pong size hail. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated supercell storm developed along a mid-level frontal boundary as a weak wave helped to increase and focus the low-level jet. With fairly weak low-level stability, severe winds easily reached the surface along the track of the storm as it transitioned to a linear structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust measured by automated surface observing system (KHON). Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal and precipitation mainly below normal during the month. Some improvement in drought conditions were observed by month's end across portions of Minnehaha and Lincoln Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought continued through August, with less than one inch of precipitation until the very end of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal and precipitation mainly below normal during the month. Some improvement in drought conditions were observed by month's end across portions of Minnehaha and Lincoln Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought continued through August, with a brief worsening late month to extreme (D3) conditions after a 12-day period mid-month without precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were slightly above normal and precipitation mainly below normal during the month. Some improvement in drought conditions were observed by month's end across portions of Minnehaha and Lincoln Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought continued through August, with only around 50 percent of normal precipitation for the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near to around two degrees above normal during the month, while precipitation was spotty, and generally varied from 50 to 100 percent of normal for most areas. Parts of far southeast South Dakota received near 150 percent of normal precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought continued through most of July, with conditions moderating to severe (D2) drought for a brief period mid-month following a 10-day period of below normal temperatures and over 3 inches of precipitation at Gregory. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near to around two degrees above normal during the month, while precipitation was spotty, and generally varied from 50 to 100 percent of normal for most areas. Parts of far southeast South Dakota received near 150 percent of normal precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought continued through July, spurred on by precipitation which was near normal with temperatures 2.4 degrees above normal at Alexandria. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near to around two degrees above normal during the month, while precipitation was spotty, and generally varied from 50 to 100 percent of normal for most areas. Parts of far southeast South Dakota received near 150 percent of normal precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought worsened to extreme (D3) drought early in July, spurred on by a week of temperatures nearly 7 degrees above normal with little precipitation in Wessington Springs.  A 19 day stretch occurred with no precipitation through the mid- to late-month period. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall, precipitation near to a little above normal across southeast South Dakota led to the alleviation of severe drought for some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near normal precipitation through mid-September at Montrose and 5 miles northeast of Salem allowed extreme (D3) drought conditions to improve to severe (D2) drought at mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near to around two degrees above normal during the month, while precipitation was spotty, and generally varied from 50 to 100 percent of normal for most areas. Parts of far southeast South Dakota received near 150 percent of normal precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought moderated to severe (D2) drought by mid-July as temperatures cooled with a period of almost 1.50 inches of precipitation at Yankton. The final 17 days of the month measured only 0.02 inch precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary.  With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were measured by South Dakota RWIS site SD547 3 miles southeast of Montrose. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms which developed across western South Dakota during the evening of June 10 translated eastward through the overnight hours.  An increasing low-level jet enhanced shear, with an environment strongly supportive of developing outflow pools with DCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg.  Storms congealed into a linear bowing structure through the late overnight and early morning hours, producing numerous instances of strong to damaging wind gusts.  One (direct) injury occurred in Yankton when a tree fell on an elderly man at a senior housing complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm outflow winds took down numerous branches and several tree limbs up to 5 to 6 inches in diameter across most of the city of Huron. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid degradation in drought status occurred in June as temperatures averaged 5 to 6 degrees above normal and precipitation varied between 25 and 50 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed early in the month, and was sustained by continued lack of precipitation and extreme heat (0.57 inches/9 days of 10 plus degrees above normal near Forestburg). Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed late in the day north of a warm frontal boundary across far northern Nebraska.  With elevated instability around 2000 J/kg and deep layer effective shear of 40 knots, storms took on a brief supercell structure and favored large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid degradation in drought status occurred in June as temperatures averaged 5 to 6 degrees above normal and precipitation varied between 25 and 50 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought continued through June, sustained by a few more weeks of low precipitation and extreme heat (0.40 inches/9 days of 10 degrees or more above normal at Vermillion and 0.41 inches at Akron). Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall, precipitation near to a little above normal across southeast South Dakota led to the alleviation of severe drought for some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures at Brookings were around 3 degrees above normal during September with precipitation near normal, which led to a continuation of severe (D2) drought conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures and precipitation through mid-September at Wessington Springs were near normal, and allowed extreme (D3) drought conditions to improve to severe (D2) drought at mid-month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota.  An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite weaker pre-storm deep-layer shear on the order of 25-30 knots and fairly narrow instability profiles of 1000-1500 J/kg, modifications on the storm scale and an increase in the low-level jet improved the environment enough to support several long-lived supercell clusters along a weak frontal boundary. Storms moving through the Sioux Falls area forced an early cancellation of the Sioux Empire Fair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unprecedented storm system lifted across the central Plains from December 15 into the early hours of December 16. Kinematic fields were at the top of the climatological distributions, leading to extreme wind shear in the lowest levels. Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes.||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force. Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 50 mph during the evening and into the overnight, with a peak wind gust of 65 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System near Beresford (station SD508).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 59 mph was recorded at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System near Howard (station SD597).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure built southward out of Canada, led by a weak wave of low pressure which increased the surface pressure gradient, and dropped a swath of light snowfall across southeast South Dakota and parts of southwest Minnesota. The coldest wind chill across southeast South Dakota generally ranged from 30 to 40 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -31F at Gregory (AWOS site K9D1). The cold wind chills continued into January.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -35F at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System 2 miles west of Dell Rapids (site SD520), but the coldest value was present for less than an hour. The cold wind chills continued into January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified on December 10 as it moved from the lee of the central Rockies into the Great Lakes.  A strong frontogenetic band developed ahead of the system, with weak instability present which resulted in a narrow and more intense band across parts of southeast South Dakota.  Snowfall from 6 to 11 inches was common along the axis of the band from south central South Dakota toward the Sioux Falls area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was moderate to heavy at times between 1100-1700CST, and produced treacherous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 6.2 inches at Madison and 6.0 inches 2 miles south of Winfred.
